summary_text:
Chief Complaint
Yearly physical exam and discussion of diabetes and hypertension management.

History of Present Illness
The patient is a 24-year-old female with a history of type 2 diabetes and hypertension. She reports increased snacking due to a new bakery job, leading to elevated blood sugar levels. Her blood pressure has been well-controlled. She has been sleeping well and reports no concerns about self-harm. She is currently taking metformin twice daily as prescribed.

Findings
Physical exam revealed normal vital signs, including a blood pressure of 128/78 mmHg. Neurological exam was intact. The patient's heart rhythm was regular, and a grade 2 systolic ejection fraction back ejection murmur was noted. Lung sounds were clear. Grip strength was equal bilaterally. Hemoglobin A1c is elevated, indicating out-of-control blood sugars.

Assessment
The patient's primary problem is poorly controlled type 2 diabetes, likely related to dietary changes. Hypertension is currently well-managed.

Plan
The patient will continue taking metformin twice daily. I recommend a dietary modification to reduce snacking and sweets, and I want the patient to check her fasting blood glucose in the morning. Follow up in two weeks to review morning blood glucose results and then again in two months to assess ongoing diabetes control. Continue the patient on lisinopril 10 mg daily.
reference_summary:
CHIEF COMPLAINT

Annual exam.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Beverly Ortiz is a 24-year-old female patient with a history of type 2 diabetes and high blood pressure. She is here today for her yearly exam.

The patient states she has been doing well. She has recently began working at a bakery this summer.

The patient states her diabetes is uncontrolled. Her blood sugars have fluctuated recently, which she attributes to her change in diet due to work. She is trying to improve her diet by removing additional sweets. She has been taking her medication as prescribed and has not missed any doses.

The patient states her blood pressure has been good despite her blood sugar levels being up and down. She has continued taking metformin twice a day.

She has been sleeping well and is able to sleep a full 8 hours. She denies thoughts of self-harm or suicidal ideations.

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

• Neurological/Psychological: Grip strength equal bilaterally. Neurologically intact.
• Neck: Supple without thyromegaly or lymphadenopathy. No carotid bruits appreciable.
Lungs are clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezes, rales, or rhonchi.
• Cardiovascular: Regular rate. Grade 2/6 systolic ejection fraction murmur. No gallops or rubs. No extra heart sounds. Good pulses.

RESULTS

Hemoglobin A1c is elevated.

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

Beverly Ortiz is a 24-year-old female patient with a history of type 2 diabetes and high blood pressure. She is here today for her yearly exam.

Diabetes mellitus type 2.
• Medical Reasoning: Her blood glucose levels have been uncontrolled. She is still taking metformin twice a day, but does admit to dietary indiscretion with an increase in her sweets intake.. Her most recent hemoglobin A1c was elevated.
• Medical Treatment: Continue metformin twice a day.
• Patient Education and Counseling: I encouraged her to check her fasting blood glucose levels each morning and call them in about 2 weeks. She will follow up in about 2 months, at which time we will discuss further treatment recommendations.

Hypertension.
• Medical Reasoning: Appears to be relatively stable.
• Medical Treatment: Continue lisinopril 10 mg once daily.

Patient Agreements: The patient understands and agrees with the recommended medical treatment plan.

section_miscategorizations.text: Hemoglobin A1c is elevated, indicating out-of-control blood sugars.
section_miscategorizations.reason: The elevated A1c value itself is a finding, but the phrase 'indicating out-of-control blood sugars' is interpretive/diagnostic synthesis and fits better in the Assessment section. This is a mild blending of data and interpretation rather than a clearly misleading placement.
